reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: separating composite risk index interpretations from species-specific gas hazards and SOC-vs-chemistry inference. Decoys are engineering-plausible but each fails via a single overreach from comparative/aggregate evidence to absolute or species-level claims. Expert discrimination is strong because correct choices require keeping multiple risk dimensions distinct and resisting common overgeneralizations.

The item cleanly tests the admissibility boundary between initiation resistance (failure temperature) and post-failure responder hazards (HF release, smoke duration), with correct options that appropriately separate these stages. Decoys are largely single-fault and plausible, but the presence of two “inadmissible” options with different rationales plus some wording/length asymmetry slightly increases guessability and reduces discriminative sharpness at the top end.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware, multidimensional safety judgment: initiation likelihood indicators (failure temperature, composite risk index) are intentionally set against enclosure-relevant consequences (HF yield, smoke duration), forcing experts to reject an “unambiguous” claim while still crediting LFP on specific axes. Decoy risk is low because the required response must explicitly separate hazard dimensions and tie them to ventilation-limited context, though the open-ended format may permit partial-credit answers that sound reasonable without fully resolving all required trade-offs.
